sentences.text: So the bodies were unloaded and then the Russian soldiers, they turned to us, the survivors and they said, well, you can go into that little town and just go into the houses.
And we said, well, how was that possible?
And they said, well, the houses are empty and they were.
And a family Stern, Mr. and Mrs.
Stern, asked us to share a house with them.
They had two daughters and the train had stopped outside a very small little town in a section of Germany called Saxony.
The name of the town is Trobitz.
At that time, it had inhabitants of about 800 people, so small.
And indeed, there were no people in that town, at least I didn't see any people in that town.
And because the Germans were very afraid of the Russians and when they anticipated that they would take over their town or their city, they fled.
So we moved into this home with Mr. and Mrs.
Stern.
Then George and I, we took a little cart and we went from house to house looking for food.
And we went into the cellar, and we found potatoes and we found tubes of cheese.
They had war rations at that time, tubes of cheese, tubes of milk.
We picked up a little lamb that we brought that home.
We talked bicycles.
I mean, it was a free-for-all.
And at one point, George told me to watch the cart with the food while he was going into another house.
And there was some noise going on at the end the street, and I was nosy.
I wanted to see what was going on, and I left the cart for a minute.
And it was gone when I came back.
And of course, George was upset, but we got another cart and started the process over again.
And it was a wonderful experience to be free, to have survived.
At the same time, it was very sad because originally-- and I got these figures out of Jacob Presser's book, Originally, there were 2,600 people on that train.
By the time we left Trobitz, there were 600.
So many people had died on the train.
Many people died after the liberation in Trobitz.
Both George and I became sick with typhus.
The Russians had set up a little hospital.
They did their best to help us, and the best way to help us was to get rid of the lice because it's lice that caused the typhus.
And so they cut my hair, and I screamed and yelled, so they left a couple of inches of hair on my head.
But both George and I did get typhus and that we survived was just phenomenal.
But it was a beautiful time of spring, and it was, again, my 10th birthday.
And I stepped outside, and I was getting better from the typhus.
And I saw this beautiful cherry tree in bloom and it was just-- it symbolized life and another start, and it was so beautiful.
But like I say, it was a sad time because so many people continued to die.
I went back to Trobitz, to the little town, as soon as communism fell in 1991.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was so gratified because there was a Jewish cemetery.
Now not all of the Jews who died there are buried in the cemetery, but many of them are.
And the people of Trobitz, especially the children, have maintained that cemetery so beautifully with flowers and shrubs.
And when I took the train from Leipzig to Trobitz, I could see from the train as I was approaching Trobitz a huge boulder.
And on that boulder you can see the Star of David.
And that is a mass grave where the train had stopped and some of the people from the train had been buried there.
And there are several such places in Trobitz.
And in 1995, I, again, went back Trobitz, and a wall was built with the names of the people who had died there.
Then after that, we stayed there from April 23 to about June.
By that time, people either had died or they had recovered.
And we were put, again, on a train, and the first stop was Leipzig.
We stayed in Leipzig because Trobitz is about 30 miles outside of Leipzig.
We stayed in Leipzig, and then I guess they asked my brother, well, where do you want to get off?
And we didn't know if my mother had survived, which she didn't.
My mother died in Stutthof right at that time, about April of 1945, of typhus.
So you found this out now?
Then?
No.
We found that out later.
But we didn't know if our mother have survived.
I mean, we knew about the convent in Holland and Mr. van Mackelenbergh.
So we went then to a place in Limburg.
That was the next stop, which is in Holland.
It's a province of Holland, Valkenburg.
And then from Valkenburg, we went to 's-Hertogenbosch.
And we knocked on the home of Mr. and Mrs. van Mackelenbergh, and of course, they couldn't believe that we had come back.
They had prayed for us and had hoped, but they hadn't heard from us for a year.
And maybe they had heard.
Now, one thing I do remember, one time or two times, we got a package from the Red Cross, a package with matzah.
Yes.
So matzah is one of my favorite foods, definitely.
But they were just unbelievable.
It was an unbelievable experience to see them again.
And Mr. van Mackelenbergh took us to a hospital in 's-Hertogenbosch, Saint John Tadeus, and we were examined.
And we didn't have any contagious diseases.
I mean, we were malnourished, of course.
And we stayed there for about two weeks, and then we went back to the convent in Eersel.
Then after that, we found out that my mother had died.
Well, I was in the rectory with Rector Wijers, and a nun came in.
And she just shouted from the door, their mother died of typhus in the camp, did not survive.
And that was all.
I didn't respond.
I didn't cry.
I didn't do anything.
And of course, I think that was probably the root of my depression later on.
And so I did have to go and seek some professional help and work that through because I had never really mourned the loss of my parents.
And that was the worst experience of all of it, the loss of my parents.
Then my aunt and uncle started to write to Rector Wijers.

And my mother, before she was sent to a concentration camp, had arranged with a lawyer in Lippstadt that in the event of her death that she requested that my aunt and uncle in Chicago should raise us.
And so they made arrangements then to send for us, and that took about two years.
I then went back to school.
I went back, sixth grade, fifth grade.
And I strike up a very nice friendship with two girls with whom I'm still very close friends with.
And then in April 14 of 1947, I said goodbye to Rector Wijers and to the nuns and to my friends there.
And that was very hard, very difficult for us, again, separation, and I came here to my aunt and uncle in Chicago.
M-hm.
And my aunt and uncle were well-meaning people.

My uncle was Catholic, a Catholic physician who had left Germany already in 1933 and had told my father to leave Germany, but my father could not believe that anything could happen to him or his family.
And so he did not leave.
But my uncle had already left in '33.
But on the way from the airport, my aunt said to my brother and me, we would like you to take on the name of Mueller instead of Levy.
Levy is a Jewish name, and you will have an easier time with a name like Mueller.
Well, that didn't quite sound right to me, but George didn't say anything, my brother.
I didn't say anything either.
But I felt like I was losing what little identity I still had, what few ties I still had with my parents.
Of course, I still was very Catholic.
My aunt and uncle sent me to a Catholic school, which I was very happy with because I was familiar with the nuns, and again, I struck up a friendship with one of my teachers, a nun.
And when I was about 16, 17, I started to have my doubts.

And I said, you know, sister, I said, it's hard for me to believe that some of the extraordinary doctrines that the Catholic religion expects a person to believe, like the Immaculate Conception and the miracles and so on.
And she said, oh, everybody has doubts.
Don't worry.
That will fall into place.
But I started, again, my search and that search lasted for a long time because I was confused about who I was and my identity, where I belonged.
And finally when I was about 40 years old, I found my way back to Judaism, and I took the name back of Levy.
In the meantime, I married, and I have two children for whom I wrote my memoirs and for whom I'm giving this oral testimony to.
But it sent me definitely on an odyssey, a search for myself, for my identity, for my roots, for my religion.
Yes.
I always loved school.
That was the place where I felt secure and I felt I knew what I had to do.
And I studied a lot.
And so the teachers thought I was OK, and that was in Holland as well as here.
I had a very good education at the Immaculata High School with the nuns.
I had four years of English, four years of math, four years of science.
I worked during my high school years.
I worked as a babysitter every Saturday evening, and I worked also as a nurse's aid in the hospital.
Now, I wasn't too keen about going into nursing.
I wanted to be a teacher, but my uncle, he thought every girl should be a nurse.
And so he wanted me to be a nurse.
And I did go into nursing.
I went to the University of Illinois, Cook County School of Nursing.
I received a diploma in nursing in 1957.
And then I married.
And then while I have the children, I took courses, maybe one course at a time, and it took me about 10 years to get my bachelor's of science in nursing.
And then another five years I got my master's of science in nursing.
And I've worked 24 years for the Chicago Public Schools as a nurse.
My son's name is Gerard Moeller.
My daughter's name is Kareen Moeller.
No, they didn't.
As a matter of fact, people at that time thought that it was better to let the subject rest, not to bring it up.
But what was especially difficult for me while I lived with my aunt and uncle-- and this was the big bone of contention with me.
My aunt was a German Jew by birth, but when she married my uncle, she converted to Catholicism.
And like many converts, they are extremely Catholic or Jewish, to whatever religion they convert.
But not only that, my aunt was enamored with everything German, everything German.
She didn't make a distinction between the Nazis and the Germans.
And I almost had a feeling that she did not want to hear about what had happened in Germany.
Only positive things were mentioned about Germany, and they went to Germany every year.
And they moved back to Germany for a while, but she was not able to establish contact again, so she came back here because my brother and I were here.

But my aunt, I think, was one of these unfortunate people who had really swallowed, been brainwashed by all of the propaganda that had been taught in Germany, and in other countries too, for generations so that she herself, I think, was not comfortable with her Jewish background.
And that's very sad.
She was very depressed for many, many years.
And I don't think that she was ever able to sort it out.
I did, but I really did not know how to tell them when they were little about Bergen-Belsen, that I did not know how to broach that subject.
As a matter of fact, I wrote a little story for them when they were a little, and I focused on the experiences in Holland mainly.
And if I did talk about the concentration camp or the experience, I spoke about the liberation in Trobitz.

But it was only later on when they were adults when my daughter studied history in college that she said to me, Ma, everybody knew in Germany what was happening with the Jews because the entire country was mobilized-- not that the people could do anything because once Hitler came to power, there was nothing you can do unless you wanted to get shot, but everybody knew, she said to me.
Right.
Well, in 1985, that was the 40th anniversary of the end of the war.
Well, before then, I am in the field of I'm a psychiatric nurse.
And you know you have to have a certain amount of therapy yourself.
And when I saw my aunt so depressed, I was fearful that maybe as time went by that I would really lapse into a depression.
So I did seek some help, and I had a wonderful psychiatrist who did help me.
And yes, I did speak about it.
I did not hide it.
They always knew that I am Jewish.
But when they were small, I was still searching so that unfortunately, I could not give them clear direction, not only that, I had married a Catholic man.
And when the marriage didn't work out and we got divorced, the divorce decree ordered me to raise the children Catholic.
And I was afraid if I did not that I would lose the children.
So they went to Catholic school and well, the Ten Commandments are the same in both religions.
I stressed that.
I stressed the belief in one God, which is also in the Catholic religion.
I stressed the importance of learning, of trying to understand, which I think is part of the Jewish tradition, the learning.

I stressed the importance of having an understanding and an empathy for those less fortunate and to not just give charity, but to also make sure that people preserve their dignity, which I think is a Jewish way of giving.
And so I taught those things.
I studied.
I read a lot about Jewish thought, Jewish philosophy, Martin Buber, Leo Baeck, but it wasn't until I was about 40 that I found a temple where I felt comfortable.
And that I found and I'm very happy about that.
Right.
Well, that was right after the fall of communism, and I wanted to go and see Bergen-Belsen.
So I went to Bergen-Belsen, and when I got there, I was looking for things to read because I didn't have to look at the pictures that were hanging there in the vestibule.
I knew what that was like.
And so I went up to the desk.
I sat there for a while.
And I went to the reception desk.
And I said, do you have any reading materials?
And he said, yes.
And then he said, have you been here before?
And I said, yes.
And he said, well, would you like to speak with our director?
And I said, of course.
And so I met Dr.
Thomas Rahe, who is director of the Bergen-Belsen memorial site.
And I spoke with him.
He is a very compassionate man with a great understanding of Jewish history, Jewish culture, and the history of the Holocaust.
What was kind of distressing, however, was that I had trouble finding Bergen-Belsen because I didn't have a car.
I took the train.
So when I got to the train station, I asked the woman, I know I have to take the train to Celle.
Is there a bus or something that goes from Sela to Bergen-Belsen?
She said, I don't know, just like that.
All right.
Then I got to Celle, which is the next largest town to Bergen-Belsen.
It's about 10 kilometers away from Bergen-Belsen.
When I got to Celle, I asked at the train station before I left there, is there a bus that goes to Bergen-Belsen, the memorial site?
I don't know.
So I thought, my goodness, here I am in Celle, and I can't get to Bergen-Belsen.
So I went to the post office, which is right next door to train station, and that woman was very helpful.
And she said, yes, there is a bus that is right outside here of the post office.
So I took the bus to Bergen.
And then I had to transfer, and I took another bus to Belsen.
And on the way to Belsen, I was sitting next to an older woman, about 70 at that time, and we got off together.
And she said to me, do you live here?
And I said, no, I'm visiting the Bergen-Belsen memorial site.
And she said, oh.
She said, what happened there was terrible, but none of us knew.
When I went back in 1995, that was the 50th anniversary, I was asked to speak at about eight high schools in Celle, in Landkreis Celle.
And when I got to Celle and they had a memorial service at Bergen-Belsen, the man from the culture department, he asked me, do you think that people knew what was going on in Bergen-Belsen?
And I said, I'm not sure.
And he said to me, everybody knew because there were people who went into the camp to repair things, to bring potatoes, not for us, to bring the turnips that they made soup out of.
Everybody knew, especially in Celle, what went on.
So I spoke to the students there, and they asked me, do you hate all Germans?
And I said, no, I don't hate all Germans.
I have known a lot of Germans who've been very good, my uncle.
And I would be like, the Nazis, if I hated all Germans.
I make a distinction between those murderers and criminals who killed 6 million of our people and many Germans and many homosexuals and many gypsies.
I make a distinction between those people and all Germans.
But there are many lessons to learn from the Holocaust.
Well, the students in Germany also asked me, well, they ask me how I feel.
How did it feel to go back to Bergen-Belsen?
How do you feel about being a Holocaust survivor?
Are you ashamed or are you proud?
And so I explained to them that I am neither ashamed nor do I feel guilty.
